sections.content:

Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 204 actions; a strong score needs about 71 or fewer. You are 2.9x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 201 actions returned the board to a state already seen 201 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 201 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Let me check the player's new position after the second UP action. The player was at row 45-47 and should have moved up again. Let me also check if the player is now touching or close to the center blue block.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check player position after second UP
def get_player_pos(frame):
    seg = frame.segmentation
    for node in seg['nodes']:
        if node['color'] == 'N':
            return node['boundary']
    return None

prev_player = get_player_pos(previous_frame)
curr_player = get_player_pos(current_frame)
print("Player before:", prev_player)
print("Player after:", curr_player)

# Check all objects and their positions
print("\nAll objects:")
for node in current_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
    if node['color'] not in ['w', 'P', 'c']:  # skip background, pink bar, and charcoal borders
        print(f"  {node['color']}: boundary={node['boundary']}, pixels={node['pixels']}")

# Check adjacency to see what's near the player
print("\nAdjacency list:")
for adj in current_frame.segmentation['adjacency_list']:
    i, j = adj
    ci = current_frame.segmentation['nodes'][i]['color']
    cj = current_frame.segmentation['nodes'][j]['color']
    if ci == 'N' or cj == 'N':
        print(f"  Player adjacent to: {ci} <-> {cj} (nodes {i}, {j})")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I can see the player moved from row 45-47 to row 41-43. The player is at [[41, 32], [41, 35], [43, 35], [43, 32]]. The white strip W is at [[40, 32], [40, 35]] and the player is adjacent to it.
Looking at the objects:
- Blue block (b) at [25,45]-[26,46] - top right, 2x2
- Blue block (b) at [28,28]-[31,39] - this is a large 4x11 block (28 pixels) - the platform
- Gray (g) at [29,29]-[30,38] - 2x10, inside the blue platform
- Blue block (b) at [29,17]-[30,18] - left, 2x2
- Dark gray (G) at [36,32]-[39,35] - 4x4 block
- Blue block (b) at [37,33]-[38,34] - 2x2, inside the dark gray block
- White strip (W) at [40,32]-[40,35] - 1x4, just above the player
- Player (N) at [41,32]-[43,35] - 3x4
The player is now adjacent to the white strip W. The white strip is at row 40, player is at rows 41-43. The dark gray block G is at rows 36-39 with the blue block b at rows 37-38.
So the layout from top to bottom:
- Row 25-26: blue block (top right)
- Row 28-31: large blue platform with gray interior
- Row 29-30: blue block (left)
- Row 36-39: dark gray block with blue center
- Row 40: white strip
- Row 41-43: player
The player moved up 4 rows again. If I continue moving UP, the player might reach the dark gray block G. Let me try moving UP a few more times to see what happens.
Actually, let me think about what the goal might be. The game seems to involve moving the player to touch different colored blocks. The player is currently adjacent to the white strip W. Maybe the goal is to collect all the colored blocks or reach a specific target.
Let me try moving UP again to see if the player can reach the dark gray block.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player moved from 45-47 to 41-43 (another 4 rows up)
# White strip W is at row 40, player is at 41-43
# Dark gray block G is at rows 36-39
# Let me continue moving UP to reach the dark gray block

# The player is at col 32-35, dark gray block is at col 32-35
# They're aligned horizontally, so moving UP should bring them together
action(['UP'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
